source_metadata.title_raw: Jeffrey A. Walter - A-19-128- June 21, 2019 - Sonoma

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 21, 2019

Jeffrey A. Walter
City Attorney
670 W. Napa St. Suite F
Sonoma, CA 95476

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-128

Dear Mr. Walter:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Sonoma City Manager, Cathy
Capriola regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

Does City Manager Cathy Capriola's interest in her residential real property preclude her
from participating in or influencing governmental decision-making pertinent to the proposed zoning
ordinance to permit commercial cannabis businesses?

CONCLUSION

No. It is not reasonably foreseeable the proposed zoning ordinance decision before the City
Council will have a material financial effect on the City Manager's residential real property.

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-128
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City of Sonoma is a general law city located in the County of Sonoma. The City is
approximately two square miles in area. Its population is estimated to be above 11,000. Except for
deliveries of medicinal cannabis products from licensed businesses located outside of the City's
boundaries, all other adult-use and medicinal commercial cannabis activities are currently
prohibited in all zones throughout the City.

The City Council will soon consider the adoption of a proposed ordinance ("ordinance")
amending the City's zoning code (in Sonoma, called the "development code") which will permit
the establishment and operation of no more than four, specified commercial cannabis businesses;
provided that such businesses first obtain, among other things, a use permit. None of the
commercial cannabis businesses permitted under the proposed amendment will be permitted as
of right, and must go through an extensive application process which includes an internal city
committee, approval by the City Council, and approval of a City cannabis business permit.

The proposed ordinance allows no more than one store-front retail dispensary to be located
only in the City's Commercial (C) zoning district. It would allow for one non-store front retail
dispensary, one testing laboratory and one non-volatile manufacturing business limited to activities
permitted under a State License Type N or P, to be conditionally permitted in the C zoning district
and the Mixed Use (MX) zoning district. The proposed ordinance identifies four "buffer" areas
(typically surrounding schools, day care centers, and the historic town plaza) in which no
commercial cannabis activities will be permitted irrespective of whether that buffer area includes C
and/or MX zoned parcels.

Adoption of the ordinance does not approve the establishment of any commercial
cannabis business. Adoption of the ordinance simply identifies the areas in the City in which such
businesses may, at some future time, be permitted after having successfully undertaken and
completed extensive review and permitting processes. There are 408 parcels in the MX zone and
411 parcels in the C zone. These zoning districts are generally clustered around Highway 12. Any
of these parcels are theoretical candidates for the establishment of such businesses (except that the
one store-front retail business must be located in the C zoning district). No applications are
currently at issue. Adoption of the ordinance simply identifies the areas in the City in which such
businesses may, at some future time, be permitted after having successfully undertaken and
completed extensive review and permitting processes.

The City Manager's residence is located in the southern part of Sonoma. The neighborhood
is dominated by single family homes. A small park is located across the street and to the west of the
City Manager's property. Her property is located about 800 feet from, and to the west of, State
Highway 12 (called Broadway) which traverses through the heart of the City. Her ownership
interest in her home is in excess of $2,000.00.

The City Manager's property is located over 2,500 feet from the nearest C zoned parcel. It is
also located more than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet from a number of parcels zoned MX, but
under the proposed ordinance all of those MX-zoned parcels are located within a 600-foot buffer
area that surrounds several adjacent schools. As a result, none of the MX-zoned parcels within

File No. A-19-128
Page No. 3

1,000 feet of her residence would be eligible for a cannabis business location under the proposed
ordinance.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of the Act if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's interests, including an interest in real property. (Section 87103.)

A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation
18701(b).) The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision. The City Manager's real property interest in not explicitly involved in the
decision. Accordingly, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. (Id.) If the financial result cannot be
expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.

Real Property

Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standard for real property interests, which
varies depending on the proximity of the decision's impacts to the official's property interest.
The City Manager's home is located more than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet from a number of
parcels zoned MX. All of those MX-zoned parcels are located within the delineated buffer area,
where commercial cannabis activities would continue to be prohibited under the proposed
ordinance. While you note that the ordinance would not change the zoning for any properties within
1,000 feet of the City Manager's residence because they are located within a buffer zone, any
decision on the ordinance necessarily includes a decision on the scope of the buffer zones
themselves. As such, the decision involves property located more than 500 feet, but less than 1,000
feet from the City Manager's residence.

Regulation 18702.2(a)(8) states materiality is found where the decision involves property
more than 500 feet, but less than 1,000 feet from the official's parcel, and would change the
development, income producing potential, or highest and best use of the official's parcel; or if it
would change the character of the official's parcel by substantially altering traffic levels, intensity
of use, parking, views, privacy, noise levels, or air quality.

You note that the City Manager's property is located in a neighborhood dominated by single
family homes, and is over 2,500 feet from the nearest C zoned parcel, where commercial cannabis
activities would be permitted under the proposed ordinance. While the property, is also located
more than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet from a number of parcels zoned MX the decision before
the City is limited to the approval of a zoning ordinance to permit commercial cannabis businesses.

2 We note that the facts give no indication of the role the City Manager may play in this particular decision and
whether that role would rise to "participating in or influencing a governmental decision" as defined in Regulation
18704(b) and (c).

File No. A-19-128
Page No. 4

The proposed ordinance allows no more than one store-front retail dispensary to be located only in
the City's C zoning district. It would also only allow for one non-store front retail dispensary, one
testing laboratory and one non-volatile manufacturing business to be conditionally permitted in the
C zoning district and the MX zoning district. At most, this ordinance would allow a total of four
cannabis related businesses to operate within commercial and mixed-use zones in the City. The
ordinance does not approve the establishment or specific location of any commercial cannabis
business, which would only happen after an extensive review and permitting processes. Due to the
limited scope of the changes proposed to the permitted uses in existing commercial and mix uses
zones, there is no indication from the facts presented that this decision will have any effect on the
City Manager's residential property. Therefore, it is not reasonable foreseeable that the decision
will have a material effect on the City Manager's residence.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By:    Zachary W. Norton
       Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:aja

sections.question:
Does City Manager Cathy Capriola's interest in her residential real property preclude her 
from participating in or influencing governmental decision-making pertinent to the proposed zoning 
ordinance to permit commercial cannabis businesses?

No. It is not reasonably foreseeable the proposed zoning ordinance decision before the City 
Council will have a material financial effect on the City Manager's residential real property.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does City Manager Cathy Capriola's interest in her residential real property preclude her 
from participating in or influencing governmental decision-making pertinent to the proposed zoning 
ordinance to permit commercial cannabis businesses?

CONCLUSION: No. It is not reasonably foreseeable the proposed zoning ordinance decision before the City 
Council will have a material financial effect on the City Manager's residential real property.
